title: Pisa

infobox.value: [Calambrone](/wiki/Calambrone "Calambrone"), [Coltano](/wiki/Coltano "Coltano"), [Marina di Pisa](/wiki/Marina_di_Pisa "Marina di Pisa"), [San Piero a Grado](/wiki/San_Piero_a_Grado,_Pisa "San Piero a Grado, Pisa"), [Tirrenia](/wiki/Tirrenia "Tirrenia")

([/ˈpiːzə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*PEE-zə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); Italian: [[ˈpiːza]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:It-Pisa.ogg "File:It-Pisa.ogg") or [[ˈpiːsa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")[[3]](#cite_note-3)) is a city and *[comune](/wiki/Comune "Comune")* (municipality) in [Tuscany](/wiki/Tuscany "Tuscany"), [Central Italy](/wiki/Central_Italy "Central Italy"), straddling the [Arno](/wiki/Arno "Arno") just before it empties into the [Ligurian Sea](/wiki/Ligurian_Sea "Ligurian Sea"). It is the capital city of the [Province of Pisa](/wiki/Province_of_Pisa "Province of Pisa"). Although Pisa is known worldwide for the [Leaning Tower of Pisa](/wiki/Leaning_Tower_of_Pisa "Leaning Tower of Pisa"), the city contains more than twenty other historic churches, and several medieval and Renaissance palaces, mostly facing each other on the avenues along the Arno ("Lungarno"). Much of the city's architecture was financed from its history as one of the Italian [maritime republics](/wiki/Maritime_republics "Maritime republics").

**Pisa** ([/ˈpiːzə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*PEE-zə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); Italian: [[ˈpiːza]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:It-Pisa.ogg "File:It-Pisa.ogg") or [[ˈpiːsa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")[[3]](#cite_note-3)) is a city and *[comune](/wiki/Comune "Comune")* (municipality) in [Tuscany](/wiki/Tuscany "Tuscany"), [Central Italy](/wiki/Central_Italy "Central Italy"), straddling the [Arno](/wiki/Arno "Arno") just before it empties into the [Ligurian Sea](/wiki/Ligurian_Sea "Ligurian Sea"). It is the capital city of the [Province of Pisa](/wiki/Province_of_Pisa "Province of Pisa"). Although Pisa is known worldwide for the [Leaning Tower of Pisa](/wiki/Leaning_Tower_of_Pisa "Leaning Tower of Pisa"), the city contains more than twenty other historic churches, and several medieval and Renaissance palaces, mostly facing each other on the avenues along the Arno ("Lungarno"). Much of the city's architecture was financed from its history as one of the Italian [maritime republics](/wiki/Maritime_republics "Maritime republics").

The city is also home to the [University of Pisa](/wiki/University_of_Pisa "University of Pisa"), which has a history going back to the 12th century, the [Scuola Normale Superiore di Pisa](/wiki/Scuola_Normale_Superiore_di_Pisa "Scuola Normale Superiore di Pisa"), founded by Napoleon in 1810, and its offshoot, the [Sant'Anna School of Advanced Studies](/wiki/Sant%27Anna_School_of_Advanced_Studies "Sant'Anna School of Advanced Studies").[[4]](#cite_note-gradscuola/-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Pisa](/wiki/Timeline_of_Pisa "Timeline of Pisa").

### Ancient times

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ancient times")]

Most believe the hypothesis that the origin of the name Pisa comes from [Etruscan](/wiki/Etruscan_language "Etruscan language") and means 'mouth', as Pisa is at the mouth of the Arno river.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Although throughout history there have been several uncertainties about the origin of the city of Pisa, excavations made in the 1980s and 1990s found numerous archaeological remains, including the fifth century BC tomb of an Etruscan prince, proving the [Etruscan](/wiki/Etruscan_civilization "Etruscan civilization") origin of the city, and its role as a maritime city, showing that it also maintained trade relations with other Mediterranean civilizations.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[Ancient Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") authors referred to Pisa as an old city. [Virgil](/wiki/Virgil "Virgil"), in his *[Aeneid](/wiki/Aeneid "Aeneid")*, states that Pisa was already a great center by the times described; and gives the epithet of *Alphēae* to the city because it was said to have been founded by colonists from [Pisa](/wiki/Pisa,_Greece "Pisa, Greece") in [Elis](/wiki/Elis "Elis"), near which the [Alpheius river](/wiki/Alfeios "Alfeios") flowed.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The Virgilian commentator [Servius](/wiki/Maurus_Servius_Honoratus "Maurus Servius Honoratus") wrote that the Teuti founded the town 13 centuries before the start of the common era.

The maritime role of Pisa should have been already prominent if the ancient authorities ascribed to it the invention of the [naval ram](/wiki/Ram_bow "Ram bow"). Pisa took advantage of being the only port along the western coast between [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa") (then a small village) and [Ostia](/wiki/Ostia_Antica_(archaeological_site) "Ostia Antica (archaeological site)"). Pisa served as a base for Roman naval expeditions against [Ligurians](/wiki/Liguri "Liguri") and [Gauls](/wiki/Gauls "Gauls"). In 180 BC, it became a Roman colony under Roman law, as *Portus Pisanus*. In 89 BC, *Portus Pisanus* became a *[municipium](/wiki/Municipium "Municipium")*. Emperor [Augustus](/wiki/Caesar_Augustus "Caesar Augustus") fortified the colony into an important port and changed the name to *Colonia Iulia obsequens*.

Pisa supposedly was founded on the shore, but due to the alluvial sediments from the Arno and the Serchio, whose mouth lies about 11 km (7 mi) north of the Arno's, the shore moved west. Strabo states that the city was 4.0 km (2.5 mi) away from the coast. Currently, it is located 9.7 km (6 mi) from the coast. However, it was a maritime city, with ships sailing up the Arno.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In the 90s AD, a [baths complex](/wiki/Baths_of_Nero_(Pisa) "Baths of Nero (Pisa)") was built in the city.

### Late Antiquity and Early Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Late Antiquity and Early Middle Ages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Pisa_Map_V_century_a.C..jpg/250px-Pisa_Map_V_century_a.C..jpg)](/wiki/File:Pisa_Map_V_century_a.C..jpg)

Hypothetical map of Pisa in the fifth century AD

During the last years of the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire"), Pisa did not decline as much as the other cities of Italy, probably due to the complexity of its river system and its consequent ease of defence. In the seventh century, Pisa helped [Pope Gregory I](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_I "Pope Gregory I") by supplying numerous ships in his military expedition against the [Byzantines](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") of [Ravenna](/wiki/Ravenna "Ravenna") (what "military expedition by Pope Gregory against the Byzantine Empire"[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]): Pisa was the sole Byzantine centre of [Tuscia](/wiki/Toscana "Toscana") to fall peacefully in [Lombard](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards") hands, through assimilation with the neighbouring region where their trading interests were prevalent. Pisa began in this way its rise to the role of main port of the Upper Tyrrhenian Sea and became the main trading centre between Tuscany and [Corsica](/wiki/Corsica "Corsica"), [Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia"), and the southern coasts of France and Spain.

After [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") had defeated the Lombards under the command of [Desiderius](/wiki/Desiderius "Desiderius") in 774, Pisa went through a crisis, but soon recovered. Politically, it became part of the duchy of [Lucca](/wiki/Lucca "Lucca"). In 860, Pisa was captured by [vikings](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings") led by [Björn Ironside](/wiki/Bj%C3%B6rn_Ironside "Björn Ironside"). In 930, Pisa became the county centre (status it maintained until the arrival of [Otto I](/wiki/Otto_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Otto I, Holy Roman Emperor")) within the mark of [Tuscia](/wiki/Tuscia "Tuscia"). Lucca was the capital but Pisa was the most important city, as in the middle of tenth century [Liutprand of Cremona](/wiki/Liutprand_of_Cremona "Liutprand of Cremona"), bishop of [Cremona](/wiki/Cremona "Cremona"), called Pisa *Tusciae provinciae caput* ("capital of the province of [Tuscia](/wiki/Tuscia "Tuscia")"), and a century later, the marquis of Tuscia was commonly referred to as "marquis of Pisa". In 1003, Pisa was the protagonist of the first [communal](/wiki/Medieval_commune "Medieval commune") war in Italy, against Lucca. From the naval point of view, since the ninth century, the emergence of the [Saracen](/wiki/Saracen "Saracen") pirates prompted the city to expand its fleet. In the following years, this fleet gave the town an opportunity for more expansion. In 828, Pisan ships assaulted the coast of [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"). In 871, they took part in the defence of [Salerno](/wiki/Salerno "Salerno") from the Saracens. In 970, they gave also strong support to [Otto I](/wiki/Otto_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Otto I, Holy Roman Emperor")'s expedition, defeating a Byzantine fleet in front of [Calabrese](/wiki/Calabria "Calabria") coasts.

### 11th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 11th century")]

Main article: [Republic of Pisa](/wiki/Republic_of_Pisa "Republic of Pisa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Pisa_Map_XI_century_b.C..jpg/250px-Pisa_Map_XI_century_b.C..jpg)](/wiki/File:Pisa_Map_XI_century_b.C..jpg)

Hypothetical map of Pisa in the 11th century AD

The power of Pisa as a maritime nation began to grow and reached its apex in the 11th century, when it acquired traditional fame as one of the four main historical [maritime republics](/wiki/Maritime_republics "Maritime republics") of Italy (*[Repubbliche Marinare](/wiki/Repubbliche_Marinare "Repubbliche Marinare")*).

At that time, the city was a very important commercial centre and controlled a significant [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean") merchant fleet and navy. It expanded its powers in 1005 through the sack of *[Reggio Calabria](/wiki/Reggio_Calabria "Reggio Calabria")* in the south of Italy. Pisa, which was attacked in 1011,[[9]](#cite_note-9) was in continuous conflict with some '[Saracens](/wiki/Saracen "Saracen")' - a medieval term to refer to Arab [Muslims](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") - who had their bases in Corsica. In 1017, [Sardinian](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia") [Giudicati](/wiki/Giudicati "Giudicati") were militarily supported by Pisa, in alliance with Genoa, to defeat the Saracen King Mugahid, who had settled a logistic base in the north of Sardinia the year before. This victory gave Pisa supremacy in the [Tyrrhenian Sea](/wiki/Tyrrhenian_Sea "Tyrrhenian Sea"). When the Pisans subsequently ousted the Genoese from Sardinia, a new conflict and rivalry was born between these major marine republics. Between 1030 and 1035, Pisa went on to defeat several rival towns in Sicily and conquer [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage") in [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"). In 1051–1052, the admiral Jacopo Ciurini conquered [Corsica](/wiki/Corsica "Corsica"), provoking more resentment from the Genoese. In 1063, Admiral Giovanni Orlandi, coming to the aid of the [Norman](/wiki/Normans "Normans") [Roger I](/wiki/Roger_I_of_Sicily "Roger I of Sicily"), took [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo") from the Saracen pirates. The gold treasure taken from the Saracens in Palermo allowed the Pisans to start the building of their cathedral and the other monuments which constitute the famous *[Piazza del Duomo](/wiki/Piazza_del_Duomo,_Pisa "Piazza del Duomo, Pisa")*.

In 1060, Pisa engaged in its first battle with [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa"). The Pisan victory helped to consolidate its position in the Mediterranean. [Pope Gregory VII](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_VII "Pope Gregory VII") recognised in 1077 the new "Laws and customs of the sea" instituted by the Pisans, and in 1081 Emperor [Henry IV](/wiki/Henry_IV,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Henry IV, Holy Roman Emperor") recognized their communal government.[[10]](#cite_note-10) This was simply a confirmation of the present situation, because in those years, the marquis had already been excluded from power. In 1092, [Pope Urban II](/wiki/Pope_Urban_II "Pope Urban II") awarded Pisa the supremacy over Corsica and Sardinia, and at the same time raising the town to the rank of archbishopric.

The Pisan fleet captured the [Zirid](/wiki/Zirid_dynasty "Zirid dynasty") city of [Mahdia](/wiki/Mahdia "Mahdia") in 1087, securing a ransom from its ruler.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Four years later, Pisan and Genoese ships helped [Alfonso VI of Castilla](/wiki/Alfonso_VI_of_Castilla "Alfonso VI of Castilla") to push [El Cid](/wiki/El_Cid "El Cid") out of [Valencia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Valencia "Kingdom of Valencia"). A Pisan fleet of 120 ships also took part in the [First Crusade](/wiki/First_Crusade "First Crusade"), and the Pisans were instrumental in the taking of [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") in 1099.[[12]](#cite_note-:0-12) On their way to the [Holy Land](/wiki/Holy_Land "Holy Land"), the ships did not miss the occasion to sack some Byzantine islands; the Pisan crusaders were led by their archbishop [Daibert](/wiki/Daibert "Daibert"), the future [patriarch of Jerusalem](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Patriarch_of_Jerusalem "Greek Orthodox Patriarch of Jerusalem"). Pisa and the other *Repubbliche Marinare* took advantage of the crusade to establish trading posts and colonies in the Eastern coastal cities of the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant"). In particular, the Pisans founded colonies in [Antiochia](/wiki/Antioch "Antioch"), Acre, [Jaffa](/wiki/Jaffa "Jaffa"), [Tripoli](/wiki/Tripoli,_Lebanon "Tripoli, Lebanon"), [Tyre](/wiki/Tyre_(Lebanon) "Tyre (Lebanon)"), [Latakia](/wiki/Latakia "Latakia"), and Accone. They also had other possessions in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") and [Caesarea](/wiki/Caesarea_Maritima "Caesarea Maritima"), plus smaller colonies (with lesser autonomy) in [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo"), [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria"), and of course [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople"), where the [Byzantine Emperor](/wiki/Byzantine_Emperor "Byzantine Emperor") [Alexius I Comnenus](/wiki/Alexius_I_Comnenus "Alexius I Comnenus") granted them special mooring and trading rights. In all these cities, the Pisans were granted privileges and immunity from taxation, but had to contribute to the defence in case of attack. In the 12th century, the Pisan quarter in the eastern part of Constantinople had grown to 1,000 people. For some years of that century, Pisa was the most prominent commercial and military ally of the Byzantine Empire, overcoming [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice") itself.

### 12th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 12th century")]

In 1113, Pisa and [Pope Paschal II](/wiki/Pope_Paschal_II "Pope Paschal II") set up, together with the count of [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona") and other contingents from [Provence](/wiki/Provence "Provence") and Italy (Genoese excluded), [a war to free the Balearic Islands](/wiki/1113-1115_Balearic_Islands_expedition "1113-1115 Balearic Islands expedition") from the [Moors](/wiki/Moors "Moors"); the queen and the king of [Mallorca](/wiki/Mallorca "Mallorca") were brought in chains to Tuscany. Though the [Almoravides](/wiki/Almoravides "Almoravides") soon reconquered the island, the booty taken helped the Pisans in their magnificent programme of buildings, especially the [cathedral](/wiki/Piazza_del_Duomo,_Pisa "Piazza del Duomo, Pisa"), and Pisa gained a role of pre-eminence in the [Western Mediterranean](/wiki/Western_Mediterranean "Western Mediterranean").

In the following years, the powerful Pisan fleet, led by archbishop [Pietro Moriconi](/wiki/Pietro_Moriconi "Pietro Moriconi"), drove away the Saracens after ferocious battles. Though short-lived, this Pisan success in Spain increased the rivalry with Genoa. Pisa's trade with [Languedoc](/wiki/Languedoc "Languedoc"), [Provence](/wiki/Provence "Provence") ([Noli](/wiki/Noli "Noli"), [Savona](/wiki/Savona "Savona"), [Fréjus](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9jus "Fréjus"), and [Montpellier](/wiki/Montpellier "Montpellier")) were an obstacle to Genoese interests in cities such as [Hyères](/wiki/Hy%C3%A8res "Hyères"), [Fos](/wiki/Fos-sur-Mer "Fos-sur-Mer"), [Antibes](/wiki/Antibes "Antibes"), and [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille").

The war began in 1119 when the Genoese attacked several galleys on their way home to the motherland, and lasted until 1133. The two cities fought each other on land and at sea, but hostilities were limited to raids and pirate-like assaults.

In June 1135, [Bernard of Clairvaux](/wiki/Bernard_of_Clairvaux "Bernard of Clairvaux") took a leading part in the [Council of Pisa](/wiki/Council_of_Pisa_(1135) "Council of Pisa (1135)"), asserting the claims of Pope [Innocent II](/wiki/Innocent_II "Innocent II") against those of Pope [Anacletus II](/wiki/Anacletus_II "Anacletus II"), who had been elected pope in 1130 with [Norman](/wiki/Normans "Normans") support, but was not recognised outside Rome. Innocent II resolved the conflict with Genoa, establishing Pisan and Genoese spheres of influence. Pisa could then, unhindered by Genoa, participate in the conflict of Innocent II against king [Roger II of Sicily](/wiki/Roger_II_of_Sicily "Roger II of Sicily"). [Amalfi](/wiki/Amalfi "Amalfi"), one of the maritime republics (though already declining under Norman rule), was conquered on August 6, 1136; the Pisans destroyed the ships in the port, assaulted the castles in the surrounding areas, and drove back an army sent by Roger from [Aversa](/wiki/Aversa "Aversa"). This victory brought Pisa to the peak of its power and to a standing equal to Venice. Two years later, its soldiers sacked [Salerno](/wiki/Salerno "Salerno").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Pisa.Campo.wall.jpg/250px-Pisa.Campo.wall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pisa.Campo.wall.jpg)

New city walls, erected in 1156 by Consul Cocco Griffi

In the following years, Pisa was one of the staunchest supporters of the [Ghibelline](/wiki/Ghibelline "Ghibelline") party. This was much appreciated by [Frederick I](/wiki/Frederick_Barbarossa "Frederick Barbarossa"). He issued in 1162 and 1165 two important documents, with these grants: Apart from the jurisdiction over the Pisan countryside, the Pisans were granted freedom of trade in the whole empire, the coast from [Civitavecchia](/wiki/Civitavecchia "Civitavecchia") to [Portovenere](/wiki/Portovenere "Portovenere"), a half of [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo"), [Messina](/wiki/Messina "Messina"), [Salerno](/wiki/Salerno "Salerno") and [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples"), the whole of [Gaeta](/wiki/Gaeta "Gaeta"), [Mazara](/wiki/Mazara_del_Vallo "Mazara del Vallo"), and [Trapani](/wiki/Trapani "Trapani"), and a street with houses for its merchants in every city of the [Kingdom of Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sicily "Kingdom of Sicily"). Some of these grants were later confirmed by [Henry VI](/wiki/Henry_VI,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Henry VI, Holy Roman Emperor"), [Otto IV](/wiki/Otto_IV "Otto IV"), and [Frederick II](/wiki/Frederick_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor"). They marked the apex of Pisa's power, but also spurred the resentment of other cities such as [Lucca](/wiki/Lucca "Lucca"), [Massa](/wiki/Massa,_Tuscany "Massa, Tuscany"), [Volterra](/wiki/Volterra "Volterra"), and [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), thwarting their aim to expand towards the sea. The clash with Lucca also concerned the possession of the castle of [Montignoso](/wiki/Montignoso "Montignoso") and mainly the control of the *[Via Francigena](/wiki/Via_Francigena "Via Francigena")*, the main trade route between Rome and France. Last, but not least, such a sudden and large increase of power by Pisa could only lead to another war with Genoa.

Genoa had acquired a dominant position in the markets of southern France. The war began in 1165 on the [Rhône](/wiki/Rh%C3%B4ne "Rhône"), when an attack on a convoy, directed to some Pisan trade centres on the river, by the Genoese and their ally, the count of [Toulouse](/wiki/Toulouse "Toulouse"), failed. Pisa, though, was allied to Provence. The war continued until 1175 without significant victories. Another point of attrition was [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily"), where both the cities had privileges granted by [Henry VI](/wiki/Henry_VI,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Henry VI, Holy Roman Emperor"). In 1192, Pisa managed to conquer Messina. This episode was followed by a series of battles culminating in the Genoese conquest of [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Italy "Syracuse, Italy") in 1204. Later, the trading posts in Sicily were lost when the new [Pope Innocent III](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_III "Pope Innocent III"), though removing the [excommunication](/wiki/Excommunication "Excommunication") cast over Pisa by his predecessor [Celestine III](/wiki/Pope_Celestine_III "Pope Celestine III"), allied himself with the [Guelph League](/wiki/Guelphs_and_Ghibellines "Guelphs and Ghibellines") of Tuscany, led by Florence. Soon, he stipulated[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] a pact with Genoa, too, further weakening the Pisan presence in southern Italy.

To counter the Genoese predominance in the southern Tyrrhenian Sea, Pisa strengthened its relationship with its traditional Spanish and French bases (Marseille, [Narbonne](/wiki/Narbonne "Narbonne"), [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"), etc.) and tried to defy the [Venetian](/wiki/Republic_of_Venice "Republic of Venice") rule of the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea"). In 1180, the two cities agreed to a nonaggression treaty in the Tyrrhenian and the Adriatic, but the death of Emperor [Manuel Comnenus](/wiki/Manuel_Comnenus "Manuel Comnenus") in Constantinople changed the situation. Soon, attacks on Venetian convoys were made. Pisa signed trade and political pacts with [Ancona](/wiki/Ancona "Ancona"), [Pula](/wiki/Pula "Pula"), [Zara](/wiki/Zadar "Zadar"), [Split](/wiki/Split,_Croatia "Split, Croatia"), and [Brindisi](/wiki/Brindisi "Brindisi"); in 1195, a Pisan fleet reached Pola to defend its independence from Venice, but the Serenissima soon reconquered the rebel sea town.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Piazza_del_Duomo_Pisa_7.jpg/960px-Piazza_del_Duomo_Pisa_7.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piazza_del_Duomo_Pisa_7.jpg "View of the Piazza dei Miracoli")

View of the [Piazza dei Miracoli](/wiki/Piazza_dei_Miracoli "Piazza dei Miracoli")

One year later, the two cities signed a peace treaty, which resulted in favourable conditions for Pisa, but in 1199, the Pisans violated it by blockading the [port of Brindisi](/wiki/Port_of_Brindisi "Port of Brindisi") in [Apulia](/wiki/Apulia "Apulia"). In the following naval battle, they were defeated by the Venetians. The war that followed ended in 1206 with a treaty in which Pisa gave up all its hopes to expand in the Adriatic, though it maintained the trading posts it had established in the area. From that point on, the two cities were united against the rising power of Genoa and sometimes collaborated to increase the trading benefits in Constantinople.

### 13th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 13th century")]

In 1209 in [Lerici](/wiki/Lerici "Lerici"), two councils for a final resolution of the rivalry with Genoa were held. A 20-year peace treaty was signed, but when in 1220, the emperor [Frederick II](/wiki/Frederick_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor") confirmed his supremacy over the [Tyrrhenian](/wiki/Tyrrhenian_Sea "Tyrrhenian Sea") coast from [Civitavecchia](/wiki/Civitavecchia "Civitavecchia") to [Portovenere](/wiki/Portovenere "Portovenere"), the Genoese and Tuscan resentment against Pisa grew again. In the following years, Pisa clashed with Lucca in [Garfagnana](/wiki/Garfagnana "Garfagnana") and was defeated by the [Florentines](/wiki/Florence "Florence") at Castel del Bosco. The strong [Ghibelline](/wiki/Ghibelline "Ghibelline") position of Pisa brought this town diametrically against the Pope, who was in a dispute with the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), and indeed the pope tried to deprive Pisa of its dominions in northern [Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia").

In 1238, [Pope Gregory IX](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_IX "Pope Gregory IX") formed an alliance between Genoa and Venice against the empire, and consequently against Pisa, too. One year later, he excommunicated Frederick II and called for an anti-Empire council to be held in Rome in 1241. On May 3, 1241, a combined fleet of Pisan and Sicilian ships, led by the emperor's son [Enzo](/wiki/Enzio_of_Sardinia "Enzio of Sardinia"), attacked a Genoese convoy carrying prelates from northern Italy and France, next to the isle of [Giglio](/wiki/Isola_del_Giglio "Isola del Giglio") ([Battle of Giglio](/wiki/Battle_of_Giglio_(1241) "Battle of Giglio (1241)")), in front of [Tuscany](/wiki/Tuscany "Tuscany"); the Genoese lost 25 ships, while about a thousand sailors, two cardinals, and one bishop were taken prisoner. After this major victory, the council in Rome failed, but Pisa was excommunicated. This extreme measure was only removed in 1257. Anyway, the Tuscan city tried to take advantage of the favourable situation to conquer the Corsican city of [Aleria](/wiki/Aleria,_Corsica "Aleria, Corsica") and even lay siege to Genoa itself in 1243.

The [Ligurian](/wiki/Liguria "Liguria") republic of Genoa, however, recovered fast from this blow and won back [Lerici](/wiki/Lerici "Lerici"), conquered by the Pisans some years earlier, in 1256.

The great expansion in the Mediterranean and the prominence of the merchant class urged a modification in the city's institutes. The system with consuls was abandoned, and in 1230, the new city rulers named a *capitano del popolo* ("people's chieftain") as civil and military leader. Despite these reforms, the conquered lands and the city itself were harassed by the rivalry between the two families of [Della Gherardesca](/wiki/Della_Gherardesca "Della Gherardesca") and [Visconti](/wiki/Visconti_of_Pisa "Visconti of Pisa"). In 1237 the archbishop and the Emperor Frederick II intervened to reconcile the two rivals, but the strains continued. In 1254, the people rebelled and imposed 12 *Anziani del Popolo* ("People's Elders") as their political representatives in the commune. They also supplemented the legislative councils, formed of noblemen, with new People's Councils, composed by the main guilds and by the chiefs of the People's Companies. These had the power to ratify the laws of the Major General Council and the Senate.

### Decline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Decline")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Nuremberg_chronicles_f_45v_1.png/250px-Nuremberg_chronicles_f_45v_1.png)](/wiki/File:Nuremberg_chronicles_f_45v_1.png)

Idealized depiction of Pisa from the 1493 *[Nuremberg Chronicle](/wiki/Nuremberg_Chronicle "Nuremberg Chronicle")*.

The decline is said to have begun on August 6, 1284, when the numerically superior fleet of Pisa, under the command of [Albertino Morosini](/wiki/Albertino_Morosini "Albertino Morosini"), was defeated by the brilliant tactics of the Genoese fleet, under the command of [Benedetto Zaccaria](/wiki/Benedetto_I_Zaccaria "Benedetto I Zaccaria") and [Oberto Doria](/wiki/Oberto_Doria "Oberto Doria"), in the dramatic naval [Battle of Meloria](/wiki/Battle_of_Meloria_(1284) "Battle of Meloria (1284)"). This defeat ended the maritime power of Pisa and the town never fully recovered;[[12]](#cite_note-:0-12) in 1290, the Genoese destroyed forever the Porto Pisano (Pisa's port), and [covered the land with salt](/wiki/Salting_the_earth "Salting the earth"). The region around Pisa did not permit the city to recover from the loss of thousands of sailors from the Meloria, while Liguria guaranteed enough sailors to Genoa. Goods, however, continued to be traded, albeit in reduced quantity, but the end came when the Arno started to change course, preventing the galleys from reaching the city's port up the river. The nearby area also likely became infested with [malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria"). The true end came in 1324, when Sardinia was entirely lost to the [Aragonese](/wiki/Aragon "Aragon").

Always Ghibelline, Pisa tried to build up its power in the course of the 14th century, and even managed to defeat [Florence](/wiki/Republic_of_Florence "Republic of Florence") in the [Battle of Montecatini](/wiki/Battle_of_Montecatini "Battle of Montecatini") (1315), under the command of [Uguccione della Faggiuola](/wiki/Uguccione_della_Faggiuola "Uguccione della Faggiuola"). Eventually, however, after a long siege, Pisa was occupied by Florentines in 1405.[[13]](#cite_note-bantam-13) Florentines corrupted the *capitano del popolo* ("people's chieftain"), Giovanni Gambacorta, who at night opened the city gate of San Marco. Pisa was never conquered by an army. In 1409, Pisa was the seat of a [council](/wiki/Council_of_Pisa "Council of Pisa") trying to set the question of the [Great Schism](/wiki/Western_Schism "Western Schism"). In the 15th century, access to the sea became more difficult, as the port was silting up and was cut off from the sea. When in 1494, [Charles VIII of France](/wiki/Charles_VIII_of_France "Charles VIII of France") invaded the Italian states to claim the [Kingdom of Naples](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Naples "Kingdom of Naples"),[[13]](#cite_note-bantam-13) Pisa reclaimed its independence as the Second Pisan Republic.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Comune_di_Pisa_1875.jpg/250px-Comune_di_Pisa_1875.jpg)](/wiki/File:Comune_di_Pisa_1875.jpg)

Bonus certificate of Pisa, issued July 19, 1875

The new freedom did not last long; 15 years of battles and sieges by the Florentine troops led by [Antonio da Filicaja](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_da_Filicaja&action=edit&redlink=1 "Antonio da Filicaja (page does not exist)"), [Averardo Salviati](/w/index.php?title=Averardo_Salviati&action=edit&redlink=1 "Averardo Salviati (page does not exist)") and [Niccolò Capponi](/wiki/Niccol%C3%B2_Capponi "Niccolò Capponi") were made, but they failed to conquer the city. [Vitellozzo Vitelli](/wiki/Vitellozzo_Vitelli "Vitellozzo Vitelli") with his brother [Paolo](/wiki/Paolo_Vitelli_(condottiero) "Paolo Vitelli (condottiero)") were the only ones who actually managed to break the strong defences of Pisa and make a breach in the Stampace bastion in the southern west part of the walls, but he did not enter the city. For that, they were suspected of treachery and Paolo was put to death. However, the resources of Pisa were getting low, and at the end, the city was sold to the Visconti family from Milan and eventually to Florence again. [Livorno](/wiki/Livorno "Livorno") took over the role of the main port of Tuscany. Pisa acquired a mainly cultural role spurred by the presence of the [University of Pisa](/wiki/University_of_Pisa "University of Pisa"), created in 1343, later reinforced by the [Scuola Normale Superiore di Pisa](/wiki/Scuola_Normale_Superiore_di_Pisa "Scuola Normale Superiore di Pisa"), founded in 1810 with a decree by [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon"), and more recently by [Sant'Anna School of Advanced Studies](/wiki/Sant%27Anna_School_of_Advanced_Studies "Sant'Anna School of Advanced Studies") (1987).

For fourteen years between 1801 and 1815, Tuscany came under French and Spanish domination. In 1801, the region became the [Kingdom of Etruria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Etruria "Kingdom of Etruria"), created from the [Grand Duchy of Tuscany](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Tuscany "Grand Duchy of Tuscany") under the [Treaty of Aranjuez](/wiki/Treaty_of_Aranjuez_(1801) "Treaty of Aranjuez (1801)"), ruled by King [Louis I of Etruria](/wiki/Louis_I_of_Etruria "Louis I of Etruria"), a cousin of the [King of Spain](/wiki/King_of_Spain "King of Spain"), as part of a larger agreement between [Napoleonic France](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") and [Spain](/wiki/History_of_Spain_(1700-1808) "History of Spain (1700-1808)"). However, after only six years, in 1807, Napoleon dissolved the kingdom and integrated it into France, with Pisa being within the new French [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") of [Méditerranée](/wiki/M%C3%A9diterran%C3%A9e_(department) "Méditerranée (department)"). But only eight years later, after the final total [defeat of Napoleon](/wiki/Campaign_in_north-east_France_(1814) "Campaign in north-east France (1814)") in 1814, the department was disbanded in 1815, under the terms of the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna"), and the Grand Duchy of Tuscany was restored to its previous [Habsburg-Lorraine](/wiki/Habsburg-Lorraine "Habsburg-Lorraine") prince, [Ferdinand III](/wiki/Ferdinand_III,_Grand_Duke_of_Tuscany "Ferdinand III, Grand Duke of Tuscany").

Pisa was the birthplace of the important early physicist [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei"). It is still the seat of an [archbishopric](/wiki/Archbishopric "Archbishopric"). Besides its educational institutions, it has become a light industrial centre and a railway hub. It suffered repeated destruction during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

Since the early 1950s, the US Army has maintained [Camp Darby](/wiki/Camp_Darby "Camp Darby") just outside Pisa, which is used by many US military personnel as a base for vacations in the area.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Climate")]

Pisa has a borderline [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Cfa*) and [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Csa*). The city is characterized by cool to mild winters and hot summers. This transitional climate allows Pisa to have summers with moderate [rainfall](/wiki/Rainfall "Rainfall"). Rainfall peaks in autumn. [Snow](/wiki/Snow "Snow") is rare. The highest officially recorded temperature was 39.5 °C (103.1 °F) on 22 August 2011 and the lowest was −13.8 °C (7.2 °F) on 12 January 1985.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### *Gioco del Ponte*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Gioco del Ponte")]

In Pisa there was a festival and game *[Gioco del Ponte](/w/index.php?title=Gioco_del_Ponte&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gioco del Ponte (page does not exist)")* [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gioco_del_Ponte "it:Gioco del Ponte"); [fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gioco_del_Ponte "fr:Gioco del Ponte")] (Game of the Bridge) which was celebrated (in some form) in Pisa from perhaps the 1200s down to 1807. From the end of the 1400s the game took the form of a mock battle fought upon Pisa's central bridge (*Ponte di Mezzo*). The participants wore quilted armor and the only offensive weapon allowed was the *targone*, a shield-shaped, stout board with precisely specified dimensions. Hitting below the belt was not allowed. Two opposing teams started at opposite ends of the bridge. The object of the two opposing teams was to penetrate, drive back, and disperse the opponents' ranks and to thereby drive them backwards off the bridge. The struggle was limited to forty-five minutes. Victory or defeat was immensely important to the team players and their partisans, but sometimes the game was fought to a draw and both sides celebrated.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In 1677 the battle was witnessed by Dutch travelling artist [Cornelis de Bruijn](/wiki/Cornelis_de_Bruijn "Cornelis de Bruijn"). He wrote:

> "While I stayed in [Livorno](/wiki/Livorno "Livorno"), I went to Pisa to witness the bridge fight there. The fighters arrived fully armored, wearing helmets, each carrying their banner, which was planted at both ends of the bridge, which is quite wide and long. The battle is fought with certain wooden implements made for this purpose, which they wear over their arms and are attached to them, with which they pummel each other so intensely that I saw several of them carried away with bloody and crushed heads. Victory consists of capturing the bridge, in the same way as the fistfights in [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice") between the [it:Castellani and the Nicolotti](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Castellani_e_Nicolotti "it:Castellani e Nicolotti")."[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In 1927 the tradition was revived by college students as an elaborate costume parade. In 1935 [Vittorio Emanuele III](/wiki/Vittorio_Emanuele_III "Vittorio Emanuele III") with the royal family witnessed the first revival of a modern version of the game, which has been pursued in the 20th and 21st centuries with some interruptions and varying degrees of enthusiasm by Pisans and their civic institutions.

## Main sights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Main sights")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Italy_-_Pisa_-_Leaning_Tower.jpg/250px-Italy_-_Pisa_-_Leaning_Tower.jpg)](/wiki/File:Italy_-_Pisa_-_Leaning_Tower.jpg)

The [Leaning Tower of Pisa](/wiki/Leaning_Tower_of_Pisa "Leaning Tower of Pisa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/PisaBaptistry20020323_rectilinear.jpg/250px-PisaBaptistry20020323_rectilinear.jpg)](/wiki/File:PisaBaptistry20020323_rectilinear.jpg)

[Pisa Baptistery](/wiki/Pisa_Baptistery "Pisa Baptistery")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Pisa-Camposanto.jpg/250px-Pisa-Camposanto.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pisa-Camposanto.jpg)

The Monumental *[Campo Santo](/wiki/Camposanto_Monumentale "Camposanto Monumentale")* in the *[Piazza del Duomo](/wiki/Piazza_del_Duomo,_Pisa "Piazza del Duomo, Pisa")*

While the [bell tower of the cathedral](/wiki/Leaning_Tower_of_Pisa "Leaning Tower of Pisa"), known as "the leaning Tower of Pisa", is the most famous image of the city, it is one of many works of art and architecture in the city's *[Piazza del Duomo](/wiki/Piazza_del_Duomo,_Pisa "Piazza del Duomo, Pisa")*, also known, since the 20th century, as *Piazza dei Miracoli* (Square of Miracles), to the north of the old town center. The *Piazza del Duomo* also houses the *[Duomo](/wiki/Duomo_di_Pisa "Duomo di Pisa")* (the Cathedral), the [Baptistry](/wiki/Baptistry_(Pisa) "Baptistry (Pisa)") and the *[Campo Santo](/wiki/Camposanto_Monumentale "Camposanto Monumentale")* (the monumental cemetery). The medieval complex includes the above-mentioned four sacred buildings, the old hospital (now *Museo delle Sinopie*) and few palaces. All the complex is kept by the *[Opera (fabrica ecclesiae)](/wiki/Opera_(fabrica_ecclesiae) "Opera (fabrica ecclesiae)") della Primaziale Pisana*, an old non profit foundation that has operated since the building of the Cathedral in 1063 to maintain the sacred buildings. The area is framed by medieval walls kept by the municipal administration.

Other sights include:

* [Knights' Square](/wiki/Knights%27_Square_(Pisa) "Knights' Square (Pisa)") (*Piazza dei Cavalieri*), where the *Palazzo della Carovana*, with its façade designed by [Giorgio Vasari](/wiki/Giorgio_Vasari "Giorgio Vasari") may be seen. Sited on the square.
* [Santo Stefano dei Cavalieri](/wiki/Santo_Stefano_dei_Cavalieri,_Pisa "Santo Stefano dei Cavalieri, Pisa"), church sited on Piazza dei Cavalieri, and also designed by [Vasari](/wiki/Giorgio_Vasari "Giorgio Vasari"). It had originally a single nave; two more were added in the 17th century. It houses a [bust](/wiki/Bust_(sculpture) "Bust (sculpture)") by [Donatello](/wiki/Donatello "Donatello"), and paintings by Vasari, [Jacopo Ligozzi](/wiki/Jacopo_Ligozzi "Jacopo Ligozzi"), [Alessandro Fei](/wiki/Alessandro_Fei_(painter) "Alessandro Fei (painter)"), and [Pontormo](/wiki/Jacopo_Chimenti "Jacopo Chimenti"). It also contains spoils from the many naval battles between the Cavalieri (Knights of St. Stephan) and the Turks between the 16th and 18th centuries, including the Turkish battle pennant hoisted from [Ali Pacha](/wiki/M%C3%BCezzinzade_Ali_Pasha "Müezzinzade Ali Pasha")'s flagship at the 1571 [Battle of Lepanto](/wiki/Battle_of_Lepanto_(1571) "Battle of Lepanto (1571)").
* [St. Sixtus](/wiki/San_Sisto,_Pisa "San Sisto, Pisa"). This small church, consecrated in 1133, is also close to the Piazza dei Cavalieri. It was used as a seat of the most important notarial deeds of the town, also hosting the Council of Elders. It is today one of the best preserved early [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") buildings in town.
* [St. Francis](/wiki/San_Francesco,_Pisa "San Francesco, Pisa"). The church of San Francesco may have been designed by [Giovanni di Simone](/w/index.php?title=Giovanni_di_Simone&action=edit&redlink=1 "Giovanni di Simone (page does not exist)"), built after 1276. In 1343 new chapels were added and the church was elevated. It has a single nave and a notable belfry, as well as a 15th-century cloister. It houses works by [Jacopo da Empoli](/wiki/Jacopo_da_Empoli "Jacopo da Empoli"), [Taddeo Gaddi](/wiki/Taddeo_Gaddi "Taddeo Gaddi") and [Santi di Tito](/wiki/Santi_di_Tito "Santi di Tito"). In the Gherardesca Chapel are buried [Ugolino della Gherardesca](/wiki/Ugolino_della_Gherardesca "Ugolino della Gherardesca") and his sons.
* [San Frediano](/wiki/San_Frediano,_Pisa "San Frediano, Pisa"). This church, built by 1061, has a [basilica](/wiki/Basilica "Basilica") interior with three aisles, with a crucifix from the 12th century. Paintings from the 16th century were added during a restoration, including works by [Ventura Salimbeni](/wiki/Ventura_Salimbeni "Ventura Salimbeni"), [Domenico Passignano](/wiki/Domenico_Passignano "Domenico Passignano"), [Aurelio Lomi](/wiki/Aurelio_Lomi "Aurelio Lomi"), and [Rutilio Manetti](/wiki/Rutilio_Manetti "Rutilio Manetti").
* [San Nicola](/wiki/San_Nicola_di_Pisa "San Nicola di Pisa"). This medieval church built by 1097, was enlarged between 1297 and 1313 by the [Augustinians](/wiki/Augustinians "Augustinians"), perhaps by the design of [Giovanni Pisano](/wiki/Giovanni_Pisano "Giovanni Pisano"). The octagonal belfry is from the second half of the 13th century. The paintings include the *Madonna with Child* by [Francesco Traini](/wiki/Francesco_Traini "Francesco Traini") (14th century) and *St. Nicholas Saving Pisa from the Plague* (15th century). Noteworthy are also the wood sculptures by Giovanni and [Nino Pisano](/wiki/Nino_Pisano "Nino Pisano"), and the [Annunciation](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation") by [Francesco di Valdambrino](/wiki/Francesco_di_Valdambrino "Francesco di Valdambrino").
* [Santa Maria della Spina](/wiki/Santa_Maria_della_Spina "Santa Maria della Spina"). A small white marble church alongside the Arno, is attributed to Lupo di Francesco (1230), is another excellent Gothic building.
* [San Paolo a Ripa d'Arno](/wiki/San_Paolo_a_Ripa_d%27Arno "San Paolo a Ripa d'Arno"). The church was founded around 952 and enlarged in the mid-12th century along lines similar to those of the cathedral. It is annexed to the [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") Chapel of St. Agatha, with an unusual pyramidal cusp or peak.

[San Pietro in Vinculis](/wiki/San_Pietro_in_Vinculis_(Pisa) "San Pietro in Vinculis (Pisa)"). Known as *San Pierino*, it is an 11th-century church with a crypt and a [cosmatesque](/wiki/Cosmatesque "Cosmatesque") mosaic on the floor of the main nave.

* *Borgo Stretto*. This medieval borgo or neighborhood contains strolling arcades. It includes the Gothic-Romanesque church of *San Michele in Borgo* (990).
* There are at least two other leaning towers in the city; one at the southern end of central *Via Santa Maria* (close to the San Nicola church), the other halfway through the *Piagge* riverside promenade, belltower to San Michele church.
* Medici Palace. The palace was once a possession of the Appiano family, who ruled Pisa in 1392–1398. In 1400 the Medici acquired it, and [Lorenzo de' Medici](/wiki/Lorenzo_de%27_Medici "Lorenzo de' Medici") sojourned here.
* *[Orto botanico di Pisa](/wiki/Orto_botanico_di_Pisa "Orto botanico di Pisa")*. The botanical garden of the University of Pisa is Europe's oldest university [botanical garden](/wiki/Botanical_garden "Botanical garden").
* The *Lungarno*, the avenues along the river Arno, host many relevant buildings:
* *Palazzo Reale*. The ("Royal Palace"), once belonged to the [Caetani](/wiki/Caetani "Caetani") patrician family. Here [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei") showed to [Grand Duke of Tuscany](/wiki/Grand_Duke_of_Tuscany "Grand Duke of Tuscany") the planets he had discovered with his telescope. The edifice was erected in 1559 by [Baccio Bandinelli](/wiki/Baccio_Bandinelli "Baccio Bandinelli") for [Cosimo I de Medici](/wiki/Cosimo_I_de_Medici "Cosimo I de Medici"), and was later enlarged including other palaces. The palace is now a museum.
* [Palazzo Gambacorti](/wiki/Palazzo_Gambacorti "Palazzo Gambacorti"): 14th-century Gothic palace, and now houses the offices of the municipality. The interior shows frescoes boasting Pisa's sea victories.
* [Palazzo Agostini](/wiki/Palazzo_Agostini,_Pisa "Palazzo Agostini, Pisa"). 15th-century Gothic palace that incorporates city walls predating 1155. It houses [Caffè dell'Ussero](/wiki/Caff%C3%A8_dell%27Ussero "Caffè dell'Ussero"), a coffeeshop founded in 1775, and during the 19th-century a meeting spot for patriots and intelligentsia.
* Mural *Tuttomondo*. A modern mural, the last public work by [Keith Haring](/wiki/Keith_Haring "Keith Haring"), on the rear wall of the convent of the Church of *Sant'Antonio*, painted in June 1989.

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Museums")]

* *Museo dell'Opera del Duomo*: exhibiting among others the original sculptures of [Nicola Pisano](/wiki/Nicola_Pisano "Nicola Pisano") and [Giovanni Pisano](/wiki/Giovanni_Pisano "Giovanni Pisano"), the Islamic [Pisa Griffin](/wiki/Pisa_Griffin "Pisa Griffin"), and the treasures of the cathedral.
* *Museo delle Sinopie*: showing the [sinopias](/wiki/Sinopia "Sinopia") from the Campo Santo, the monumental cemetery. These are the [underdrawings](/wiki/Underdrawing "Underdrawing") for frescoes, made with reddish, greenish or brownish earth colour with water.
* [Museo Nazionale di San Matteo](/wiki/Museo_Nazionale_di_San_Matteo "Museo Nazionale di San Matteo"): exhibiting sculptures and paintings from the 12th to 15th centuries, among them the masterworks of [Giovanni](/wiki/Giovanni_Pisano "Giovanni Pisano") and [Andrea Pisano](/wiki/Andrea_Pisano "Andrea Pisano"), the [Master of San Martino](/wiki/Master_of_San_Martino "Master of San Martino"), [Simone Martini](/wiki/Simone_Martini "Simone Martini"), [Nino Pisano](/wiki/Nino_Pisano "Nino Pisano") and [Masaccio](/wiki/Masaccio "Masaccio").
* *Museo Nazionale di Palazzo Reale*: exhibiting the belongings of the families that lived in the palace: paintings, statues, armors, etc.
* *Museo Nazionale degli Strumenti per il Calcolo*: exhibiting a collection of instruments used in science, between a pneumatic machine of [Van Musschenbroek](/wiki/Pieter_van_Musschenbroek "Pieter van Musschenbroek") and a compass which probably belonged to [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei").
* *[Museo di storia naturale dell'Università di Pisa](/wiki/Natural_History_Museum_of_the_University_of_Pisa "Natural History Museum of the University of Pisa")* (Natural History Museum of the University of Pisa), located in the [Certosa di Calci](/wiki/Certosa_di_Pisa "Certosa di Pisa"), outside the city. It houses one of the largest cetacean skeletons collection in Europe.
* *[Palazzo Blu](/wiki/Palazzo_Blu "Palazzo Blu")*: temporary exhibitions and cultural activities center, located in the Lungarno, in the heart of the old town, the palace is easy recognizable because it is the only blue building.
* [Cantiere delle Navi di Pisa](/wiki/Museum_of_Ancient_Ships,_Pisa "Museum of Ancient Ships, Pisa") - The Pisa's Ancient Ships Archaeological Area: A museum of 10,650 square meters – 3,500 archaeological excavation, 1,700 laboratories and one restoration center – that visitors can visit with a guided tour.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The Museum opened in June 2019[[24]](#cite_note-24) and has been located inside to the 16th-century Medicean Arsenals in Lungarno Ranieri Simonelli, restored under the supervision of the Tuscany [Soprintendenza](/wiki/Soprintendenza "Soprintendenza").[[25]](#cite_note-25) It hosts a remarkable collection of ceramics and [amphoras](/wiki/Amphoras "Amphoras") dated back from the [8th century BCE](/wiki/8th_century_BCE "8th century BCE") to the [2nd century BC](/wiki/2nd_century_BC "2nd century BC"),[[26]](#cite_note-26) and also 32 ships dated back from the second century BCE and the seventh century BC.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Four of them are integrally preserved[[28]](#cite_note-28) and the best one is the so-called *Barca C*, also named *[Alkedo](/wiki/Alkedo_(ship) "Alkedo (ship)")* (written in the [ancient Greek characters](/wiki/Greek_alphabet "Greek alphabet")).[[29]](#cite_note-29) The first boat was accidentally discovered in 1998 near the [Pisa San Rossore railway station](/wiki/Pisa_San_Rossore_railway_station "Pisa San Rossore railway station") and the archeological excavations were completed 20 years later.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Football")]

[Football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") is the main sport in Pisa; the local team, [Pisa SC](/wiki/Pisa_SC "Pisa SC"), currently[[31]](#cite_note-31) plays in the [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") (the highest football division in Italy), and has had a top flight history throughout the 1980s and the 1990s, featuring several world-class players such as [Diego Simeone](/wiki/Diego_Simeone "Diego Simeone"), [Christian Vieri](/wiki/Christian_Vieri "Christian Vieri") and [Dunga](/wiki/Dunga "Dunga") during this time. The club play at the [Arena Garibaldi](/wiki/Arena_Garibaldi_%E2%80%93_Stadio_Romeo_Anconetani "Arena Garibaldi – Stadio Romeo Anconetani"), which opened in 1919 and has a capacity of 25,000.

### Shooting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Shooting")]

[Shooting](/wiki/Shooting_sports "Shooting sports") was one of the first sports to have their own association in Pisa. The *Società del Tiro a Segno* di Pisa was founded on July 9, 1862. In 1885, they acquired their own training field. The shooting range was almost completely destroyed during World War II.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Airport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Airport")]

Pisa has an international airport known as [Pisa International Airport](/wiki/Pisa_International_Airport "Pisa International Airport") or normally [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei") located in San Giusto neighbourhood in Pisa. It is served by several airlines which connect it to various domestic and international destinations. Many [low-cost airlines](/wiki/Low-cost_carrier "Low-cost carrier") operate there.

### Pisamover

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Pisamover")]

The airport is connected to [Pisa Centrale railway station](/wiki/Pisa_Centrale_railway_station "Pisa Centrale railway station") by a [people mover](/wiki/People_mover "People mover") system 2 km (1.2 mi) long, called **Pisamover**[[32]](#cite_note-32) inaugurated in March 2017.[[33]](#cite_note-33) It is based on a driverless "horizontal [funicular](/wiki/Funicular "Funicular")" that travels the distance in 5 minutes, with a 5-minute frequency, having an intermediate stop at parking station San Giusto/Aurelia.

### Buses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Buses")]

**Consorzio Pisano Trasporti**, also known as **CPT**, was a [Società consortile a responsabilità limitata](/wiki/Societ%C3%A0_consortile_a_responsabilit%C3%A0_limitata "Società consortile a responsabilità limitata") (Scarl) that operated since 2005 the local public transport in Pisa and in the [province](/wiki/Province_of_Pisa "Province of Pisa"). Became subsidiary of [Compagnia Toscana Trasporti Nord](/wiki/Compagnia_Toscana_Trasporti_Nord "Compagnia Toscana Trasporti Nord")[[34]](#cite_note-34) in 2012 and was one the companies of the consortium [ONE Scarl](/wiki/ONE_Scarl "ONE Scarl")[[35]](#cite_note-35) to accomplish the contract stipulated with the [Regione Toscana](/wiki/Regione_Toscana "Regione Toscana") for the public transport in the 2018-2019 period. The fleet consisted of 70 urban, 15 suburban and 260 intercity buses.

Since 1 November 2021 the public local transport is managed by [Autolinee Toscane](/wiki/Autolinee_Toscane "Autolinee Toscane").[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### Trains

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Trains")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Stazione_di_pisa_centrale.JPG/250px-Stazione_di_pisa_centrale.JPG)](/wiki/File:Stazione_di_pisa_centrale.JPG)

[Pisa Centrale railway station](/wiki/Pisa_Centrale_railway_station "Pisa Centrale railway station")

The city is served by two railway stations available for passengers: [Pisa Centrale](/wiki/Pisa_Centrale_railway_station "Pisa Centrale railway station") and [Pisa San Rossore](/wiki/Pisa_San_Rossore_railway_station "Pisa San Rossore railway station").

*Pisa Centrale* is the main railway station and is located along the [Tyrrhenic railway line](/wiki/Pisa%E2%80%93Livorno%E2%80%93Rome_railway "Pisa–Livorno–Rome railway"). It connects Pisa directly with several other important Italian cities such as [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa"), [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin"), [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples"), [Livorno](/wiki/Livorno "Livorno"), and [Grosseto](/wiki/Grosseto "Grosseto").

*Pisa San Rossore* links the city with Lucca (20 minutes north-east of Pisa) and [Viareggio](/wiki/Viareggio "Viareggio") and is also reachable from *Pisa Centrale*. It is a minor railway station located near the Leaning Tower zone.

There was another station called [Pisa Aeroporto](/wiki/Pisa_Aeroporto_railway_station "Pisa Aeroporto railway station") situated next to the Airport with services to [Pisa Centrale](/wiki/Pisa_Centrale_railway_station "Pisa Centrale railway station") and [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"). It was closed on December 15, 2013, for the realization of a [people mover](/wiki/People_mover "People mover").

### Motorway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Motorway")]

Pisa is connected to [Autostrada A11](/wiki/Autostrada_A11_(Italy) "Autostrada A11 (Italy)") from [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence") and to [Autostrada A12](/wiki/Autostrada_A12_(Italy) "Autostrada A12 (Italy)") linking [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa")-[Rosignano](/wiki/Rosignano_Marittimo "Rosignano Marittimo") with exit Pisa Nord and Pisa Centro – [Airport](/wiki/Pisa_airport "Pisa airport").

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Education")]

Pisa hosts the [University of Pisa](/wiki/University_of_Pisa "University of Pisa"), especially renowned in the fields of [Physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics"), [Mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics"), [Engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering") and [Computer Science](/wiki/Computer_Science "Computer Science"). The *[Scuola Superiore Sant'Anna](/wiki/Sant%27Anna_School_of_Advanced_Studies "Sant'Anna School of Advanced Studies")* and the *[Scuola Normale Superiore](/wiki/Scuola_Normale_Superiore "Scuola Normale Superiore")*, the Italian academic élite institutions are noted mostly for research and the education of [graduate students](/wiki/Graduate_student "Graduate student").

Construction of a new leaning tower of glass and steel 57 meters tall, containing offices and apartments was scheduled to start in summer 2004 and take 4 years. It was designed by [Dante Oscar Benini](/w/index.php?title=Dante_Oscar_Benini&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dante Oscar Benini (page does not exist)") and raised criticism.

* The [Scuola Normale Superiore di Pisa](/wiki/Scuola_Normale_Superiore_di_Pisa "Scuola Normale Superiore di Pisa") was founded in 1810, by Napoleonic decree, as a branch of the [École Normale Supérieure](/wiki/%C3%89cole_Normale_Sup%C3%A9rieure "École Normale Supérieure") of Paris. Recognized as a "national university" in 1862, one year after Italian unification, and named during that period as "Normal School of the Kingdom of Italy" ([Superior Graduate Schools in Italy](/wiki/Superior_Graduate_Schools_in_Italy "Superior Graduate Schools in Italy") i.e. Scuola Superiore Universitaria).

Located at: Scuola Normale Superiore di Pisa – Piazza dei Cavalieri, 7 – 56126 Pisa (Italia)

* The [Sant'Anna School of Advanced Studies of Pisa](/wiki/Sant%27Anna_School_of_Advanced_Studies_of_Pisa "Sant'Anna School of Advanced Studies of Pisa") or [Scuola Superiore Sant'Anna](http://www.sssup.it/) is a special-statute public university located in Pisa, Italy, emerging from *Scuola Normale Superiore di Pisa* and operating in the field of applied sciences, ([Superior Graduate Schools in Italy](/wiki/Superior_Graduate_Schools_in_Italy "Superior Graduate Schools in Italy") i.e. Scuola Superiore Universitaria).

Located at: Scuola Superiore Sant'Anna, P.zza Martiri della Libertà, 33 – 56127 – Pisa (Italia)

* The [University of Pisa](/wiki/University_of_Pisa "University of Pisa") or [Università di Pisa](https://web.archive.org/web/20190206145631/https://www.unipi.it/), is one of the oldest universities in Italy. It was formally founded on September 3, 1343, by an edict of Pope Clement VI, although there had been lectures on law in Pisa since the 11th century. The university has Europe's oldest academic botanical garden i.e. [Orto botanico di Pisa](/wiki/Orto_botanico_di_Pisa "Orto botanico di Pisa"), founded 1544. The medical school of the university is sight at [Azienda ospedaliero-universitaria pisana](/w/index.php?title=Azienda_ospedaliero-universitaria_pisana&action=edit&redlink=1 "Azienda ospedaliero-universitaria pisana (page does not exist)").

Located at: Università di Pisa – Lungarno Pacinotti, 43 – 56126 Pisa (Italia)
